file_name: Tejada et al. - 2024 - Use of xception architecture for the classification of skin lesions.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses the Xception deep convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture, implemented via the TensorFlow and Keras frameworks, to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset for the early detection of melanoma and other malignant skin conditions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and deployment of an Xception deep learning model for skin lesion classification. It details specific methods including the use of the HAM10000 dataset, data preprocessing, model training with TensorFlow/Keras, and evaluation metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score). The results section reports new performance data comparing Xception against ResNet50V2 and Inception V3, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Use of Xception Architecture for the Classification of Skin Lesions
journal: Journal of Systemics, Cybernetics and Informatics
brief_summary: This study investigates the application of the Xception deep convolutional neural network architecture for the accurate classification of skin lesions to facilitate the early detection of melanoma and other malignant conditions. The researchers utilized the HAM10000 dataset, comprising over 10,000 dermoscopic images of benign and malignant lesions, to train and evaluate the model within the TensorFlow and Keras frameworks. Preprocessing steps included data splitting, augmentation, and image resizing to address class imbalance and enhance model robustness. The model's performance was assessed using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics, comparing it against baseline models such as ResNet50V2 and Inception V3. The experimental results demonstrated that the Xception architecture outperformed the baseline models across all evaluated metrics, establishing it as a highly suitable model for this classification task. The findings underscore the potential of deep learning to improve diagnostic accuracy and support early intervention in skin cancer detection.

all_task_types.target: 7 discrete skin lesion classes (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is the 'classification of skin lesions' and 'identifying distinct skin cancer types'. The model uses a softmax activation function with 7 neurons to produce probability outputs for each of the 7 classes. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, which are standard metrics for multi-class classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A large collection of multi-source dermatoscopic images of common pigmented skin lesions, published in 2018. The original dataset contained 10,015 images, but after removing duplicates (images captured at different magnifications or angles of the same lesion), 7,470 distinct skin lesion images remained for use in the study.
classes_used: actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma/Bowen’s disease (akiec)
benign keratosis-like lesions (solar lentigines/seborrheic keratoses and lichen-planus like keratoses) (bkl)
vascular lesions (angiomas, angiokeratomas, pyogenic granulomas and hemorrhage) (vasc)

use.training: Used to train the Xception model after data splitting (60% of non-duplicate dataset) and augmentation.
use.validation: Used for unbiased evaluation of the model during training and hyperparameter tuning (20% of non-duplicate dataset).
use.testing: Used for unbiased evaluation of the final trained model (20% of non-duplicate dataset).
use.other: The pre-trained Xception base model was initialized using weights from the ImageNet dataset (14,197,122 images) via transfer learning.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.split: Test Set (20% of non-duplicate dataset, 1,494 images)
HAM10000.evaluation_setting: Comparison of Xception, ResNet50V2, and Inception V3 models
HAM10000.notes: Xception achieved the highest accuracy (81%) and F1-score (40%). Inception V3 showed the lowest accuracy (77%) and F1-score (21%). The paper notes that performance on minority classes was affected by data imbalance, with high confusion in classes 1, 4, and 5 for Xception and ResNet50V2, and classes 1, 2, and 5 for Inception V3. Class 6 (melanocytic nevi) showed a high hit rate across all models.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on ImageNet dataset (14,197,122 images) using transfer learning; base model loaded without top classification layers.

architecture_details.backbone: Xception deep convolutional neural network incorporating Depthwise Separable Convolutions and residual connections.

training_details.hardware: Ryzen 5800H CPU, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 GPU (6GB), 16 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Duplicate removal from the HAM10000 dataset (reducing 10,015 images to 7,470 distinct lesions)
Data splitting into training (60%), validation (20%), and testing (20%) sets
Pixel value rescaling to range 0-1 (dividing by 255)
Data augmentation including rotation (up to 10 degrees), zooming (0.1 range), flipping, cut out, and cropping

Random zoom with a range of 0.1 (zoom_range: 0.1).

conclusions: The Xception architecture achieved the highest performance among the tested models, with an accuracy of 81% and an F1-score of 0.40 on the HAM10000 test set.
Xception outperformed ResNet50V2 (80% accuracy, 0.39 F1-score) and Inception V3 (77% accuracy, 0.21 F1-score) in classifying skin lesions.
The study highlights the significance of using extensive and diverse datasets, as well as rigorous clinical validation, for developing deep learning models for skin cancer detection.
Performance on minority classes was affected by data imbalance, with high confusion observed in specific classes (e.g., classes 1, 4, and 5 for Xception and ResNet50V2).
Class 6 (melanocytic nevi) showed a high hit rate across all models, indicating better classification performance for this class compared to others.

The findings underscore the potential of deep learning to improve diagnostic accuracy and support early intervention in skin cancer detection, particularly in geographies with restricted access to dermatological services.
The authors acknowledge limitations related to data imbalance and suggest that more clinical images from diverse patient populations should be included to bolster diagnostic system performance.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance (e.g., 6,705 images for melanocytic nevi vs. 115 for dermatofibroma), potentially biasing performance metrics.
The dataset was pre-processed by removing duplicates (reducing 10,015 images to 7,470 distinct lesions) to prevent data leakage from multiple views of the same lesion.
Performance on minority classes was notably poor, with high confusion observed in classes 1, 4, and 5 for Xception and ResNet50V2, and classes 1, 2, and 5 for Inception V3.
The paper did not perform external validation on independent datasets; results are limited to the internal test split of the HAM10000 dataset.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported to validate the significance of performance differences between models.
The F1-scores for all models were relatively low (Xception: 0.40, ResNet50V2: 0.39, Inception V3: 0.21), indicating challenges in balancing precision and recall, particularly for imbalanced classes.
The study acknowledges that the model's performance may be affected by the unequal distribution of instances and suggests that class balancing techniques are needed for clinical applicability.
Hardware constraints were noted (Ryzen 5800H CPU, NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3060 GPU 6GB), which may limit reproducibility for larger-scale deployments without similar resources.
